title: Anomalous, pre-yield grain-boundary sliding in copper revealed with in-situ high-resolution strain mapping
authors: Benjamin Poole, David Lunt, Luke Hewitt, Chris Hardie
abstract: Grain boundary sliding is typically associated with high temperature deformation in engineering alloys. Here, we examine grain boundary sliding at room temperature in oxygen-free high-conductivity copper under quasi-static tensile testing. By using high-resolution digital image correlation (HRDIC) conducted in-situ within a scanning electron microscope to produce time-series strain maps, we unexpectedly observe that grain boundary sliding occurs extensively prior to macroscopic yield, and before the onset of significant crystallographic slip. Extreme values in strain and in-plane rotation are found to be associated with grain boundaries immediately prior to yield and during the initial stages of plastic deformation, which are higher than those associated with crystallographic slip. By combining laser scanning confocal microscopy height mapping with the strain maps and orientation maps from electron backscatter diffraction, grain boundary sliding character is determined, finding evidence of pure in-plane, pure out-of-plane and mixed-mode sliding.
summary: This paper demonstrates that grain boundary sliding in copper occurs at room temperature during the elastic regime, before the material reaches its macroscopic yield point. By using advanced in-situ microscopy techniques, the authors show that sliding can be in-plane, out-of-plane, or mixed-mode. This discovery challenges the conventional understanding that grain boundary sliding is a phenomenon reserved for high-temperature deformation. The findings provide new insights into the early stages of plastic deformation in polycrystalline metals.
main_problem: Investigating the mechanism and timing of grain boundary sliding (GBS) in copper at room temperature, specifically challenging the view that GBS is primarily a high-temperature phenomenon.
main_result: The study reveals that grain boundary sliding occurs extensively prior to macroscopic yield and before the onset of significant crystallographic slip, exhibiting pure in-plane, pure out-of-plane, and mixed-mode sliding.
method: In-situ mechanical loading within a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) using high-resolution digital image correlation (HRDIC), electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) for 3D height mapping.
model_or_system: Oxygen-free high-conductivity (OFHC) copper subjected to quasi-static tensile testing at room temperature.
key_observables: Effective strain, in-plane rotation, crystallographic slip (trans-granular bands), and height gradient magnitude (as a proxy for out-of-plane deformation).
important_parameters: Nanoscale resolution (98 nm strain field resolution), 20 um x 20 um field of view, and the distinction between pre-yield and post-yield regimes.
main_assumptions: A 1.5 um dilation of grain boundaries was required to account for spatial smearing and potential misalignments between EBSD and HRDIC maps.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Experimental workflow; Fig 2: In-situ stress-strain curve and strain/rotation maps; Fig 3: Time-series snapshots of strain evolution; Fig 4: Quantitative comparison of strain/rotation in grain cores vs. boundaries; Fig 5: Classification of sliding modes (in-plane, out-of-plane, and mixed).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of GBS timing, describes the experimental setup and multi-modal imaging workflow, presents time-series strain maps and quantitative comparisons of grain cores vs. boundaries, characterizes the 3D nature of sliding, and discusses the implications for bulk vs. surface deformation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Experimental workflow with both sample preparation and mechanical testing phases.

Figure 2: In-situ experimental output. The engineering stress-strain curve (a) shows individual automated testing steps in different colours with image montage points shown as black circles. The orientation map (b) shows orientations parallel with the primary loading direction (x- direction). (c) and (d) are the HRDIC derived effective strain and in-plane rotation maps, respectively, taken under the maximum load condition indicated with an × symbol in (a).

Figure 3: Progression of grain boundary sliding for strain around the yield point. Five snapshots of strain (yellow points in (a)) are shown for strains immediately pre- and post-yield. The yield stress here is 120 MPa. A sub-region (b) of the ROI in Figure 2 is shown here for clarity. Effective strain and in-plane rotation maps clearly show the onset of grain boundary sliding prior to macroscopic yielding, followed by increasing levels of slip post-yield. Strain values indicated here are calculated using HRDIC, by taking the mean value of the x-component for each map.

Figure 4: Strain distributions partitioned by microstructural region. Histograms (b) show the progression of effective strain for four deformation steps around the yield point. Vertical dashed lines indicate the 99.99th percentile for each region.

Figure 5: Characterisation of grain boundary activity using combined strain and height mapping. The GB activity map in (a) shows the deformation type at each grain boundary segment, including boundaries where no significant deformation was observed. Three separate line profiles have been extracted as examples of the three main grain boundary deformation modes of, (b) out-of-plane dominated sliding, (c) in-plane dominated sliding, and (d) mixed sliding.
